text:
People are now like “but what about the oil mini-glut?! 😏”

But important to stress that there was in fact a mini-glut as Hormuz exits surged and Asian (*cough* China) import demand remained weak—it was just temporary and comparatively small, hence the “mini”.

Wouldn’t have seen prompt, contango if there wasn’t at least a brief oversupply of spot-available crude. 

Even if the drive lower was mainly paper pressure, a tighter physical market would have provided a more reliable floor before we got there.

Mini-glut now appears to be passed at lease for now, moving back to scarcity signals.

But you’re doing yourself a disservice if you only believe timespreads when they agree with your prior.

(Prompt Brent futures timespread in white, Brent Dated to Frontline—DFL—spread in blue)

Holy hell, Brent prompt timespreads are RIPPING!

From mild contango last week to >$2/bbl backwardation this morning—that’s… a heck of a lot.

Kpler numbers show Iran’s onshore crude storage has cleared out by 15M barrels since the end of May, dragging inventories right back to normal operating levels.

And float of available Iranian ballast tankers sitting in the Persian Gulf might buy them a bit of a tactical window. We're talking 24 Iranian vessels parked out there right now.

While the traditionally inverse relationship between oil and gold appears to be softening, it is still too early to conclude that the correlation has broken down. It may, however, signal that the market is becoming increasingly concerned about the broader economic fallout from a sustained energy shock. Tightness in refined fuel markets has already pushed diesel and gasoline prices to levels consistent with Brent trading well above USD 100 per barrel, raising the risk that growth concerns may increasingly offset inflation and rate-related headwinds for gold.

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 15, 2026

Market Brief

China's first-half economic data, including GDP and credit figures, will be released today.

China's first-half foreign trade volume exceeded 25 trillion yuan, with AI computing power as the strongest growth driver.

Changxin Technology's IPO offering price is set at 8.66 yuan per share, with subscription to open on July 16.
U.S. forces launched a new strike against Iran and have resumed maritime blockade.

Trump reverses stance on Hormuz Strait shipping fees, proposing trade and investment deals instead of a 20% surcharge.

Kevin Warsh's debut congressional hearing: zero tolerance for persistent inflation, Fed launches "blank slate" policy review.

U.S. June CPI inflation eased, marking the first monthly decline in six years.

New York State suspends new large data center construction for one year, the first such ban in the U.S.
Global equities rose Tuesday, with memory chip stocks surging; IBM, after an earnings warning, slid over 25%.
Warren Buffett accelerates charitable giving, planning to liquidate all Berkshire Hathaway shares within eight years.

Top Stories

China's first-half economic data will be released today. The National Bureau of Statistics will announce key indicators including GDP, investment, consumption, and industrial production at 10:00 a.m. The June housing price data for 70 major cities will be released at 9:30 a.m. The State Council Information Office will hold a press conference at 10:00 a.m. with Deputy Director of the National Bureau of Statistics, Mao Shengyong to present the first-half economic performance and answer questions. The State Council Information Office will also hold a briefing at 3:00 p.m. on the first-half monetary policy implementation and financial statistics.

Changxin Technology's STAR Market IPO offering price has been set at 8.66 yuan per share, with subscription to commence on July 16. The offering is expected to raise nearly 58 billion yuan (before exercise of the over-allotment option), making it the largest IPO on the STAR Market to date. If the over-allotment option is fully exercised, total proceeds could reach approximately 66.6 billion yuan. At this price, Changxin Technology's market capitalization at listing is estimated at about 579.2 billion yuan. Strategic investors include major state-backed investment funds, insurance institutions, semiconductor industry players, and downstream application companies. 

A-share listed firms including Montage Technology, Tongfu Microelectronics, and AMEC have each been allocated 18.24 million shares, worth 158 million yuan each.

U.S. forces continued strikes against multiple sites in Iran on July 14, while Iran retaliated against U.S. military bases. The struggle for control over the Strait of Hormuz persists. U.S. President Trump stated that the U.S. will replace the proposed 20% fee on Hormuz Strait shipping with trade and investment agreements with Gulf nations. The U.S. will impose a "complete blockade" on Iran, allowing all vessels except those from Iran to transit the Strait. Iran's military has vowed not to yield on the issue. Iran's parliament has proposed legislation on Strait security.
The U.S. Central Command announced that, at 4:00 p.m. Eastern Time on July 14, it resumed maritime blockade targeting vessels entering or exiting Iranian ports and coastal areas. More than 20 U.S. Navy ships and hundreds of military aircraft are currently deployed in the Middle East.

U.S. inflation rose less than expected in June. Data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics showed that the U.S. June CPI increased 3.5% year-over-year, and core CPI rose 2.6%, both notably lower than the previous reading and below market forecasts. The U.S. June CPI fell 0.4% month-over-month—the first monthly decline in six years and far exceeding the expected 0.1% drop. After the data release, traders pushed back their expectations for a Fed rate hike to October.

Kevin Warsh, in his first semiannual congressional hearing as Fed chair, adopted a hawkish stance, declaring "zero tolerance" for five years of persistent inflation and rejecting market views that inflation is "under control" due to the June CPI decline. Warsh announced the formation of five working groups to conduct a "blank slate" review of the Fed's current policy framework. In response to pressure from the Trump administration for rate cuts, Warsh refused to provide forward guidance, emphasizing that the Fed will base decisions solely on economic data.

China Macro & Policy

According to China Customs, China's total merchandise trade reached 25.47 trillion yuan in the first half of the year, up 16.9% year-over-year—the first time the figure has surpassed 25 trillion yuan in the same period. Electronics components and computer parts—key AI hardware—accounted for 5.13 trillion yuan in trade, up 56.6%. In June alone, China's imports and exports totaled 4.78 trillion yuan, up 24.2%, marking the 17th consecutive month of growth. Rare earth exports surged 178.82% year-over-year.

Wind data shows that, in yuan terms, China's June exports rose 20.8% year-over-year (previous: 13.8%), while imports rose 29.4% (previous: 21.5%). The trade surplus widened to 859.05 billion yuan (previous: 723.98 billion yuan). In U.S. dollar terms, June exports rose 27% (previous: 19.4%), imports rose 36% (previous: 27.4%), and the trade surplus reached $125.62 billion (previous: $105.43 billion).

The People's Bank of China will conduct a 1.4 trillion yuan six-month outright reverse repurchase operation on July 15, the largest single operation since the tool's introduction. Given that 900 billion yuan of this tenor matures in the same month, the operation will result in a net injection of 500 billion yuan—the first time since February that the PBOC has increased the volume of this tenor.

Six ministries released the "15th Five-Year Plan for Solid Waste Pollution Prevention and Control," aiming for significant progress in key sectors by 2030 and full digital monitoring coverage across priority areas. This is China's first five-year special plan targeting solid waste pollution.

The Ministry of Ecology and Environment and other departments issued the "Action Plan for Water Body Protection and Governance Near Communities," targeting issues such as discolored water, foul odors, and floating debris. The goal is to eliminate black and odorous water bodies in county and township areas by 2030.

China Equities Markets

On Tuesday, A-shares staged a sharp rebound in the afternoon, closing with a V-shaped recovery. The SSE Index rose 1.36% to 3,967.13, the Shenzhen Component Index gained 2.77%, the ChiNext Index jumped 3.43%, and the Wind All China Index advanced 1.92%. Market turnover reached 2.72 trillion yuan. The AI computing hardware supply chain surged, with PCB stocks hitting multiple limit-up closures. Telecommunications equipment, precious metals, nonferrous metals, coal, and oil stocks also rose. Commercial aerospace stocks continued to decline.

Hong Kong markets ended slightly higher. The Hang Seng Index rose 0.52% to 24,340.73, the Hang Seng Tech Index gained 0.06%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index rose 0.46%. Nonferrous metals, hardware equipment, semiconductors, oil, and automotive stocks led gains. Southbound funds net bought approximately HK$11 billion, with significant additions to ZhiPu, NetEase, and Kuaishou.

New capital flooded into A-shares, with the total open interest in the CSI 1000 Index Futures (IM) contract surpassing 500,000 contracts for the first time at 500,500—setting a new record. The influx of long positions has broken the market's conventional belief that high open interest equals deep discounting, suggesting potential index recovery.

As of July 13, more than 580 foreign institutions conducted over 3,900 research visits to A-share companies this year. Electronic equipment, semiconductor equipment, and integrated circuits remain the top focus areas. Standard Chartered Bank and Goldman Sachs have issued overweight ratings on A-shares. Morgan Stanley and UBS express optimism on China's equity outlook. Chinese assets remain underweighted in global actively managed portfolios, indicating significant room for future allocation increases.

Sources indicate that DeepSeek has initiated IPO preparations and plans to submit its application by the end of this year or early next year. DeepSeek is also in preliminary discussions with new investors for a funding round, with a pre-money valuation of approximately $71 billion.

Reports suggest that SHEIN will hold its IPO hearing at the Hong Kong Stock Exchange on Thursday, aiming for a listing as early as August. SHEIN seeks to raise $2 billion to $3 billion through the offering.

China Industry News

Recent online posts from state-owned banks promote high-yield deposits claiming interest rates above 5% for large deposits. ICBC, ABC, BOC, and CCB jointly issued a risk warning to debunk the claims, stating these are fraudulent promotions for offshore insurance or asset management products.

China's National Medical Security Administration released the list of drugs passing the preliminary review for the 2026 basic medical insurance and commercial insurance drug catalog adjustments. A total of 601 drugs passed for the basic insurance list and 58 for the commercial insurance list. Passing preliminary review only grants eligibility for expert evaluation, not inclusion in the catalog.

The Ministry of Public Security reported that, as of the end of June, China's total vehicle stock reached 371 million units, of which 13.19% were new energy vehicles. In the first half of the year, 5.195 million new energy vehicles were registered, accounting for 49.42% of all new vehicle registrations.

The National Energy Administration stated that after setting a new record high of 1.518 billion kilowatts on July 10, the national power load reached another record of 1.551 billion kilowatts on July 14. Overall, China's energy supply and demand remain balanced.

Market research firms IDC, Counterpoint, and Omdia reported that global smartphone shipments declined again in the second quarter due to persistent memory shortages, hitting a multi-year low. Samsung, Apple, and Huawei posted counter-cyclical growth, while other major manufacturers saw significant declines.

Fintech

The U.S. and U.K. jointly advanced a transatlantic regulatory framework for stablecoins, releasing policy recommendations to establish clear pathways and eliminate regulatory barriers. The initiative stems from the "Future Markets Transatlantic Working Group" established in September last year, covering cross-border tokenized asset testing, regulatory coordination, and bank capital treatment for crypto-assets.

The Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation (DTCC), the U.S.'s largest securities clearinghouse, will conduct a live blockchain transaction demonstration this week involving equities and U.S. Treasuries. This marks DTCC's first live production test of tokenization processes, with full deployment targeted for October.

Corporate News & Updates

Apple Inc. received a rare downgrade to underweight from KeyBanc Capital Markets, citing concerns over demand and valuation. Apple's forward P/E ratio exceeds 33, significantly above its 10-year average of 23 and the Nasdaq 100's 22.8.

NVIDIA is considering a collaboration with Mitsubishi Heavy Industries on AI data center technology, aiming to integrate Mitsubishi's cooling and energy management systems into NVIDIA's next-generation data centers.

SK Hynix has begun ordering advanced DRAM manufacturing equipment for the first wafer fab in the Yongin semiconductor cluster. Initial production capacity is planned at 20,000 wafers per month, targeting sixth-generation 10nm-class (1c) DRAM.

SoftBank Group founder Masayoshi Son stated that future nuclear fusion technology will provide the most viable solution for AI data center energy demands. Son again dismissed "AI bubble" concerns, predicting that AI development will require $5 trillion in annual investment by 2040, with global data center capacity reaching 3 terawatts.

Sources indicate that ride-hailing giant Uber is in advanced talks with German food delivery platform Delivery Hero regarding an acquisition, with a deal potentially announced as early as this week. Delivery Hero's current market capitalization is approximately 11.9 billion euros.

Global Macro & Policy

Recent futures trading data shows that hedge funds and other investors have taken fewer long and short positions in crude oil, reducing market liquidity or the ability to trade at expected prices.

"Over the past 20 years, globalization, low-cost energy, and highly integrated supply chains have jointly created a low-inflation environment, allowing central banks to maintain low interest rates and a relatively stable macroeconomic environment. Earnings, Inflation, and Capitol Hill Take Center Stage
Hello traders and investors,

Today's session has the potential to set the tone for the rest of the week.

After a difficult start to the week that saw semiconductor stocks tumble and oil prices surge on escalating geopolitical tensions, investors now have a full slate of market-moving catalysts to digest. Major bank earnings are arriving before the opening bell, the June Consumer Price Index will provide the latest inflation update, and Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh is scheduled to testify before Congress later today.

At the same time, semiconductor stocks are attempting to recover some of Monday's losses, while higher oil prices continue creating uncertainty as developments surrounding Iran remain in focus.

Let's get you ready for the opening bell.

📊 Futures Mixed Ahead of a Busy Session

Technology is showing early signs of improvement.

While technology is attempting to recover this morning, elevated crude prices continue acting as an important counterweight for the broader market.

🏦 Earnings Season Begins in Earnest

Today marks the unofficial beginning of second-quarter earnings season.

Strong reports from the financial sector provide investors with an early look at corporate performance heading into what is expected to become a very active earnings calendar over the coming weeks.

📊 Inflation Report Takes Center Stage

The market's biggest scheduled event arrives this morning.

Economists expect June CPI to decline 0.1%.

🏛 Fed Chair Kevin Warsh Heads to Capitol Hill

Investors will also hear directly from Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh later today.

Japan and South Korea each advanced 0.7%, while Hong Kong added 0.5%.

India was the only major market to finish lower.

The improvement across much of Asia comes despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainty and continued volatility in the semiconductor industry.

💭 My Take

Today's market has no shortage of catalysts.

It's rare to see a major inflation report, Federal Reserve testimony, and the unofficial start of earnings season all arrive on the same day. Any one of those events can influence market direction. Together, they have the potential to shape expectations for the remainder of the week.

I'll be paying particularly close attention to the market's reaction rather than simply the headlines.

The three major US stock indices fell across the board, led by tech stocks—particularly the semiconductor sector—as markets were dragged down by inflation concerns triggered by escalating US-Iran geopolitical tensions and surging oil prices. 美股三大指数全线下挫，科技股尤其是半导体板块领跌，受美伊地缘政治紧张和油价暴涨引发的通胀担忧拖累。纳斯达克指数跌幅最大，结束了此前三连涨。

主要驱动因素
美伊地缘政治紧张升级

半导体与科技股集体回调  
   韩国芯片巨头 SK Hynix 在首日美股上市后暴跌约9-15%（创纪录最大单日跌幅），拖累美股芯片板块。英特尔（Intel）、美光（Micron）、AMD、ARM等芯片股普遍下跌4%-6%以上。纳斯达克因此承压最大。

其他市场亮点
银行股**：银行财报季即将开启，多家大行（如摩根大通、高盛等）出现回调。
其他板块**：能源板块因油价上涨相对抗跌；部分科技成长股（如Shopify、AppLovin）也随大盘走弱。
宏观背景**：市场正等待周二（7月14日）的6月CPI通胀数据，以及即将公布的银行财报。标普500二季度盈利预期仍较高（同比增约20.9%），但油价和地缘风险带来短期不确定性。

总体评价：7月13日美股呈现“风险厌恶”行情，地缘政治与能源价格成为主导因素，科技/AI板块出现获利了结。整体跌幅尚属可控，但短期波动性可能加大。

June CPI fell 0.4% on the month. The first negative print in six years. The annual rate came in at 3.5% against a 3.8% forecast, core at 2.6%. Within hours, the odds of a July hold jumped from 58% to 83%, short-end yields dropped, the dollar slipped, and gold got its bounce back to 4,058. PIMCO's Tiffany Wilding said the report takes a July hike effectively off the table, even if it doesn't end the tightening debate.

One thing deserves a careful look before anyone relaxes too much. This number is a photograph of June, taken while the peace talks were holding oil down. The blockade and the strikes belong to July, and they will show up in July's data. Goldman's Kay Haigh made the same point: the early-hike pressure eases, but the Iran risk hasn't gone anywhere. Oil kept saying so all day. Brent touched $85, and WTI closed just under $80 at 79.83, softening only after Trump stepped back from the Hormuz toll plan. He kept the strikes and the naval blockade. The toll was the piece that worked like a tax on energy, so walking it back took some inflation pressure out. The rest of the escalation stayed.

Beating is no longer the same thing as being rewarded, which is exactly what Samsung learned last week.

Semis bounced and carried the Nasdaq 100 up 1.1%. IBM went the other way, down 25% after warning that software and infrastructure demand is running soft. The sorting that Wells Fargo's Paul Christopher described on Monday, companies that earn from AI versus companies that spend on it, got a live example within a day.

Warsh took his first congressional testimony as Fed chair and kept it simple: the Fed will never tolerate high inflation. Edward Jones' Bryan Therrien noted he committed to nothing before more data, while still sounding firm. For Korean assets, the day helped from two sides, a softer dollar and lower yields, friendlier ground for the memory names after Monday's bruising.

Wednesday brings the producer-price index, which shows whether pipeline pressure is still building underneath, plus Warsh round two and ASML's earnings, the first real read on chip-equipment demand this season. Whether June's photograph still looks like July, tomorrow starts to answer.

Friday’s July USDA World Agricultural Supply and Demand Estimates (WASDE) report delivered a few surprises for grain traders, even with the growing season still in its early stages.

For corn, the USDA lowered its 2025/26 ending stocks estimate by 125 million bushels to 2.020 billion bushels, coming in 50 million bushels below expectations. The decline in old-crop corn ending stocks was driven by higher feed usage.

The USDA also raised its 2026/27 U.S. corn production estimate by a modest 5 million bushels from June to 16.0 billion bushels, which was in line with expectations.

Despite the slightly higher production outlook, the USDA lowered its 2026/27 U.S. corn ending stocks estimate to 1.790 billion bushels, 80 million bushels below expectations.

Global corn ending stocks for the 2026/27 season were also revised lower to 275.26 million metric tons, down from 281.22 million metric tons in the June report.

For soybeans, the USDA lowered its 2025/26 ending stocks estimate by 10 million bushels to 330 million bushels, also 10 million bushels below expectations. Stronger soybean exports drove the decline in old-crop soybean ending stocks.

The USDA raised its 2026/27 U.S. soybean production estimate by 40 million bushels from June to 4.475 billion bushels, which was 15 million bushels above expectations.

Even with the higher production estimate, the USDA lowered its 2026/27 U.S. soybean ending stocks estimate to 310 million bushels, 20 million bushels below expectations.

Global soybean ending stocks for the 2026/27 season were revised lower to 124.17 million metric tons, compared with 124.88 million metric tons in June.

Following the report, both corn and soybean prices traded modestly higher.

•  Total crude inventories (government strategic petroleum reserve + commercial/operational stocks treated as quasi-strategic): ~1.4 billion barrels (EIA and other estimates). Some sources put it slightly higher at peaks.

China’s Crude Inventories and Market Power 

This is from an article I wrote in February 2023: 
Link: https://t.co/yhtKaQjjSR

The oil market has not been competitive for about 155 years. It has been about the control of production to influence oil prices:  the market power of oil producers along with government intervention has always influenced the market.  The brief times when the market was truly competitive were marred by chaos, extreme volatility, high costs, and a decline in efficiency. 

In recent years, however, China, the world’s largest crude oil importer, started changing the game.  

Using its inventories, including the SPR, China single-handedly transformed the oil market structure from oligopoly to oligopsony. In the first, producers have market power, while in the second type of market, buyers have market power.  If the power of both sides is equal, the price will be similar to the price in the market as if it were competitive with thousands of sellers and buyers. In the case of China, it can prevent prices from increasing as it did in 2017, 2018, and 2021. And we believe that Beijing will also adopt a similar behavior in 2023.

The use of inventories makes more oil available to Chinese refiners and others, as it also reduces oil imports, and oil prices, while generating significant profits in the process. If China plays on demand seasonality, it can also prevent oil prices from rising further and lower them instead.  Once prices are low, China will then refill the SPR.

Under such a market structure where OPEC+ is an oligopoly and China is an oligopsony, OPEC sets the price floor, while China determines the price ceiling.

At present, China is awash with cheap oil from Russia and Iran and has about 891 million barrels (mb) of crude oil above the ground, according to Kpler’s data.  While the amount of oil in caverns below the ground remains unknown, it is fair to say that China’s caverns contain up to 130 mb.

Nigeria🇳🇬 has broken its OPEC production ceiling as crude oil output climbed to a 74-month high. Data from the Nigeria Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission showed the country pumped an average of 1.56 million barrels per day of crude oil in June 2026, exceeding its 1.5 million bpd OPEC quota and achieving 104 percent compliance. Including condensates, which are not subject to OPEC quotas, total oil production averaged 1.735 million bpd in June, up from 1.700 million bpd in May and marking a fourth consecutive month of growth. June’s crude oil output was the highest recorded since April 2020, a 74-month high. The regulator attributed the increase to stable operations across producing assets and the absence of major pipeline outages, which supported production uptime and crude evacuation.

This milestone follows a steady recovery after years of underperformance. In May 2026, Nigeria’s crude output hit 1.53 million bpd, surpassing the OPEC quota by 30,000 bpd and ending a 10-month stretch below target that dated back to July 2025. Combined crude and condensate production reached 1.70 million bpd in May, the highest since July 2025, and rose further in June. The turnaround reflects improved security in the Niger Delta, the restart of idle facilities, and measures to curb crude theft and pipeline vandalism that had previously cost the country 300,000 to 500,000 bpd below its quota in 2022 and 2023. With output now above quota for consecutive months, Abuja is preparing to request a higher ceiling of 2 million bpd at the November 30 OPEC meeting. Higher production could boost government revenues and foreign exchange earnings, reduce the Dangote Refinery’s dependence on crude imports, and consolidate Nigeria’s position as Africa’s largest oil producer.
